data.title: Thirty-Year-Old Code: The Battle for AI Payment Standards
data.description:
Author: Hazel Hu Source: X, @withhazelhu

Recently, AI payment has seen a surge in popularity, making a protocol standards war surrounding "how AI agents can spend money autonomously" inevitable.

In 1996, when Tim Berners-Lee and his team at CERN finalized the HTTP/1.0 specification, they left a blank space in the status code list: 402 Payment Required.

This was an interface reserved for future digital cash or micro-payment solutions. The creators of the internet foresaw thirty years ago that a toll lane would eventually be needed on the information superhighway.

... But the 402 error was never activated. No browser supported it, and no server actually used it. Like a letter with a proper address but never sent, it slumbered in the corner of the HTTP protocol for thirty years. Until AI Agent arrived. In the third week of March 2026, the payment industry saw four major events within 72 hours. On March 17, Mastercard acquired stablecoin infrastructure company BVNK for $1.8 billion. On March 18, Tempo, a public blockchain incubated by Stripe and Paradigm, officially launched its mainnet and simultaneously released the Machine Payments Protocol (MPP). On the same day, Visa Crypto Labs released visa-cli, a command-line tool that allows AI Agents to swipe cards directly on the terminal; Visa's head of crypto, Cuy Sheffield, named it "Command-Line Commerce." Prior to this, the x402 protocol, jointly launched by Coinbase and Cloudflare, had been running for nearly a year. Four pieces, one table. A long-brewing battle for standards has officially begun. The story begins with 402. Marc Andreessen once called the internet's lack of native payment capabilities "the original sin of the internet." The HTTP protocol is inherently capable of transmitting text, images, and videos, but not money. This deficiency gave rise to the entire advertising economy; since users are unwilling to pay for content, advertisers will foot the bill. Thus, we have a free but monitored internet. 402 could have changed all of this. Imagine this: your browser accesses a paid article, the server returns a 402 status code, the browser pops up a built-in micropayment interface, you click confirm, a penny is credited to your account, and the article is unlocked. The whole process is as natural as loading an image. However, the technology of the 1990s couldn't realize this vision. There were no mature solutions for digital cash, no low-cost settlement channels, and SSL and TLS were just beginning. The 402 became a placeholder ahead of its time. Thirty years later, it was awakened by a Canadian. Erik Reppel, a graduate of the University of Victoria's Computer Science department, was the head of engineering at the Web3 social company Zora before joining Coinbase. After joining Coinbase's developer platform, he began what he called "a project Coinbase has been exploring since 2015": designing a native payment standard for the internet. One of his inspirations came from the Bitcoin payment channel experiment conducted by Balaji Srinivasan, former CTO of Coinbase, during his time at 21.co. The idea behind that solution was sound, but it failed due to cost. Back then, the gas fee for each on-chain transaction was several dollars, making micropayments unworkable. However, by 2024, Layer 2 networks had reduced the cost of a single transaction to less than one ten-thousandth of a cent. The time had finally come. In May 2025, Reppel, along with colleagues Nemil Dalal and Dan Kim, jointly published a white paper, officially launching the x402 protocol. Its logic is clean and concise: a client requests a resource, the server returns an HTTP 402 response and a JSON payload specifying the price, accepted tokens, and wallet address; the client signs the payment with their wallet, settlement is completed on-chain, and the server returns the resource. The entire process takes two seconds. x402 chose the purest route: protocol-native, on-chain settlement, and no intermediaries. It's directly embedded in the HTTP layer, attempting to become part of the internet's infrastructure, like DNS and TLS. Coinbase and Cloudflare subsequently jointly established the x402 Foundation, entrusting protocol governance to a neutral foundation. What does Cloudflare's involvement mean? Approximately 20% of global network traffic passes through Cloudflare's more than 300 data centers, and x402 is integrated into its Agents SDK and MCP server. This is a distribution advantage that is difficult for any competing protocol to replicate. But another Canadian thinks differently. Liam Horne, a graduate of the University of Waterloo, entered the Ethereum field in 2016. He was classmates with Vitalik Buterin in school (they both received the Thiel Scholarship). His initial attraction to blockchain was simple: direct transfers between two pieces of software could occur without a bank. But Ethereum was too slow and too expensive. In 2017, Horne and Jeff Coleman proposed the concept of "generalized state channels" at L4 Ventures. The two parties lock their funds into a multi-signature contract, exchange signatures for transactions off-chain at any speed, and finally settle only the net result back on-chain. Zero fees, instant settlement, theoretically capable of thousands of transactions per second. They also invented a technique called "counterfactual instantiation," treating the contract as a deployed entity for interaction, even though it never actually touches the chain. This idea directly inspired Vitalik to propose EIP-1014, which later became the widely used CREATE2 opcode for account abstraction and cross-chain deployment. Horne's proudest demo is called Web3Torrent: a torrent download client where downloaders and uploaders pay small fees block-by-block through state channels. A tiny amount is paid for each downloaded file fragment. Machines pay machines, no human involvement. But the problem is—nobody needs this. From 2017 to 2020, the main theme of the Ethereum ecosystem was DeFi and transactions, not payments. State channels were technically feasible, but there were no users. It was like a tollbooth that had been built, but no cars on the highway. Horne admitted that state channels had become a "solution without problems." Rollups became a more general solution for scaling, and he turned to Optimism. At Optimism, Horne's resume rose rapidly: lead engineer, CEO, expanding the team from 8 to nearly 70, leading the development of the OP Stack, and driving the collaboration between Superchain and Base. Base is Coinbase's L2 chain. The two threads converged for the first time here. Later, Horne left OP Labs to become a consultant and also participated in the construction of World Chain. In the summer of 2024, he began intensive research on stablecoins. The success of Base made him realize one thing: the synergy between Coinbase, Circle, and Base almost replicated the stablecoin expansion path of Tether, Tron, and Binance outside the United States. Stablecoins are no longer a niche narrative; they account for 70% of all blockchain transaction volume. In September 2025, Tempo completed a $500 million Series A funding round at a $5 billion valuation, co-incubated by Stripe and Paradigm. Horne served as the head of engineering. Paradigm's CTO Georgios Konstantopoulos and co-founder Matt Huang were also deeply involved. Horne spent six years scaling Ethereum, first working on state channels, then Optimism. Then he left to work on a decentralized L1 architecture backed by a large corporation. In the same week that Tempo's mainnet launched, OP Labs announced a 20% layoff, and Vitalik had previously publicly rejected the L2 route. His classmate and fellow scholarship recipient seemed to have made his choice earlier than him. This wasn't the only intriguing instance of personnel movement. In February 2026, Dan Romero and Varun Srinivasan, co-founders of Farcaster, a decentralized social application strongly supported by Vitalik, announced they were joining Tempo. Before founding Farcaster, Romero was Coinbase's 20th employee, joining in 2014 and rising through the ranks to become VP, responsible for international and consumer business. Srinivasan also came from Coinbase, where he was responsible for engineering and product. Their departure from Farcaster was quite dramatic. After Neynar acquired the Farcaster protocol, the founding team collectively resigned, and Romero announced he would return all $180 million in funding to investors. He later wrote on X: "Stablecoins are a generation's opportunity." And that's not all. Tempo also poached Dankrad Feist, one of the key architects of the Ethereum L1 scaling roadmap, from the Ethereum Foundation. This means that Tempo's core team includes at least two former Coinbase executives, a former CEO of Ethereum L2, and a former Ethereum Foundation researcher. Meanwhile, Coinbase is pushing forward with x402. In a sense, this battle of standards is both a showdown between Coinbase alumni and a quiet split within the Ethereum ecosystem. So, what exactly does Tempo's MPP do? And how does it differ from x402? Horne's MPP protocol for Tempo can be summarized in one sentence: the Session mode of state channels has been moved to the application layer. In the world of x402, an agent completes an on-chain transaction every time it calls an API. This is fine in low-frequency scenarios, but when an AI agent needs to call the interface thousands of times within minutes, each on-chain transaction means significant friction and latency. MPP's solution is Sessions: the agent performs a handshake authentication, sets a spending limit, and then freely calls hundreds or thousands of APIs within this Session. All spending is packaged and settled at the end of the Session. Formally, this is identical to the state channels Horne implemented on Ethereum nine years ago: locked funds, off-chain interaction, and a single settlement. Another key difference is the payment method. x402 uses pure on-chain stablecoin settlement, inherently decentralized but also inherently excluding fiat currency. MPP, on the other hand, takes a "payment track-independent" approach, supporting stablecoins, credit cards, and even the Bitcoin Lightning Network. Lightning Network integration is handled by Lightspark, which itself is a Bitcoin version of "state channels." It's worth noting that Lightspark's founder is David Marcus, the former president of PayPay and a core creator of Libra. In the x402 architecture, there's another important role called the Facilitator. It's neither the buyer nor the seller, but a third-party validator sandwiched in between. After the Agent signs the payment, the money isn't directly transferred to the merchant. Instead, the Facilitator verifies the signature, confirms the balance, and executes on-chain settlement before releasing the resources. This adds an extra hop, but that extra hop handles all the dirty work: anti-fraud, compliance verification, and settlement execution. The merchant doesn't need to worry about anything; they just need to know, "The Facilitator says OK, the money has arrived." MPP eliminated this role. Horne's logic is: since both Agents and merchants will become increasingly intelligent, why is a middleman still needed? Verification, encryption, and settlement are all handled by the merchant's server. The chain is shorter, latency is lower, and there's no middleman taking a cut. But the cost is also direct. The encryption and decryption logic for card payments falls on the merchants, and the responsibility for PCI compliance also shifts there. For large merchants like Shopify and DoorDash, who have already integrated with the entire Stripe system, this isn't a problem. But what if future merchants are essentially AI agents—"zero-human companies" without compliance teams or legal departments—how will they handle this? MPP hasn't answered this question yet. Visa has also written card payment specifications for MPP. For merchants, integrating with MPP is equivalent to integrating with the existing Stripe system. Tax calculation, fraud prevention, and refund processing are all ready to use out of the box. Stripe's cold start advantage with millions of merchants worldwide is something x402 cannot match in the short term. Since its launch in May 2025, x402 officially claims to have processed over 100 million payments. Bloomberg, citing data from x402.org, reported that agents completed $24 million in payments within 30 days. However, raw data from on-chain analytics platform Allium Labs showed that the actual on-chain transaction volume during the same period was approximately $3 million. Analysts at Artemis Analytics further cleaned the data using a wash trading filter, flagging wallets that repeatedly transacted with themselves or circulated funds between addresses, ultimately arriving at a figure of $1.6 million. This discrepancy doesn't necessarily indicate a failure for x402, but it at least illustrates how early the entire agent payment sector is. What are the agents actually buying? Almost entirely pay-per-use developer tools. Firecrawl charges one cent per web crawl query, Browserbase sells browser sessions, and Freepik sells AI image generation. Users aren't after cryptocurrency; they're after the "no account registration, no subscription, just a wallet signature" feature. The author of that data analysis article did some calculations at the end: he spent a total of $0.47 using x402 to retrieve data from Allium to write the entire article. $1.6 million isn't a huge number. But none of Stripe, Cloudflare, or Google are betting on a market of $1.6 million per month. They're betting on the number after agents become the default buyers. McKinsey predicts that agent payments will reach $3 trillion to $5 trillion by 2030. Interestingly, the market hasn't taken sides. Almost all the leading players have chosen to bet on both sides. Stripe itself is both the drafter of MPP and an early integrator of x402. Anthropic is compatible with both protocols through MCP. OpenAI has a demo of MPP and is also present in the x402 ecosystem. Google didn't develop its own settlement protocol; instead, it created an authorization framework, AP2, and then directly embedded x402 into it. This illustrates that the level of this conflict is more complex than simply "two protocols fighting." This is a rational hedging strategy. Just like early internet companies supporting both HTTP and HTTPS simultaneously, not because they couldn't distinguish between the two, but because no one wanted to make the wrong bet during the migration period. However, the fundamental philosophical differences are real. x402 believes in "protocol as infrastructure." It aims to be the TCP/IP of the payment field: permissionless, chain-agnostic, accessible to anyone, and independent of any commercial entity. Currently, the Ethereum, Base, and Solana ecosystems are fully integrating x402, making it the core payment channel for the Agent-to-Agent economy. MPP believes in "infrastructure as protocol." Backed by the business empires of Stripe and Visa, it first addresses the agent needs of the traditional payment world, then gradually expands onto the blockchain. Merchants are already using Stripe for payments; adding an MPP layer allows them to accept automatic payments from agents. Some have compared them to TCP/IP and AOL in the early days of the internet. However, this analogy isn't entirely accurate. MPP is also open source and claims to be an open standard. However, as many developers have pointed out, MPP's documentation explicitly recommends Tempo as the settlement layer, and the SDK currently only supports TypeScript, while x402 already has support for multiple languages ​​such as TS, Python, and Go. One assessment is quite accurate—MPP is "a vertically integrated, non-neutral version of x402." But regardless of which protocol prevails, they both need to run on-chain, but currently, the true foundation of stablecoins doesn't lie on any new blockchain. The Ethereum mainnet, along with its Rollups (Arbitrum, Base, Optimism), carries a stablecoin supply of over $170 billion, with a monthly turnover of approximately $2.8 trillion. x402 currently primarily runs on Base and Solana, where Agent micropayments actually occur. Further down the chain, Tron processes over $600 billion in stablecoin transfers monthly, almost entirely small cross-border remittances under a thousand dollars. While not part of the Silicon Valley narrative, it is the world's largest stablecoin network in terms of usage. Building upon this existing landscape, two new chains "born for stablecoins" have simultaneously entered the market. Stripe has Tempo, and Circle has Arc; both chains aim for sub-second confirmation, predictable fees, and compliant privacy, claiming to be the next generation of payment infrastructure. However, none of them have been validated by large-scale real-world transactions; they have either just launched on the mainnet (Tempo, March 18th) or are still on the testnet (Arc, planned for mainnet launch in 2026). Two new chains are challenging three established chains and their Layer 2 infrastructures, two protocols are vying for standard positions, and multiple payment giants are each vying for their own spots. The table is becoming increasingly crowded. But at least Agent Payments are making the new challengers look formidable. Ethereum and Solana are designed for general-purpose computing; stablecoin payments can run on them, but they are not optimal. Gas fees fluctuate with network congestion, settlement certainty depends on the overall load of the chain, and compliance and privacy require additional infrastructure. Agents need certainty precisely: predictable fees down to the cent, millisecond-level finality, and out-of-the-box compliance frameworks. These three new chains were designed for these needs from the very first line of code, betting that the incremental market for agent payments is large enough to bypass the existing advantages of established chains and directly establish a foothold in this new arena. The logic behind this bet is simple: the number of agents is likely to far exceed the number of humans. While a person might swipe their card several times a day, an agent might call the API hundreds of times per second, each call representing a micro-payment. Coinbase's Brian Armstrong stated that soon the number of online agents will surpass the number of human users. However, today's agents control almost no wealth; they don't have their own wallets, independent budgets, and must consult with humans for every penny spent. The battle for standards is about reaching the critical point where agents transition from "running errands" to "spending money autonomously." Whoever lays the groundwork for this at that moment will capture this wave of traffic. Tim Berners-Lee in 1996 could never have foreseen today's situation. He left an interface for payments in the HTTP protocol, like leaving a window in a white wall. For thirty years, that window remained closed, until an AI agent poked its head in and said: "I need to pay." Multiple teams rushed in, vying to frame that window. One believed in open protocols, another was backed by a commercial empire, and still others were vying to build a trust layer between the two. Many of the people standing among them even shared the same name, emerging from the same office and heading in opposing directions. This battle for standards has only just begun. Who wins and who loses may not depend on the technology itself, but on who makes that window indispensable first. After all, in the history of the internet, it has never been the most elegant protocol that wins, but the one used by the most people. But one thing is certain: the 402 status code, dormant for thirty years, has finally awakened. And this time, it's not a human knocking on the door.

data.title: TAO invests in OpenAI, Elon Musk, and Subnet, which is Sam Altman.
data.description:
Author｜ Momir @IOSG

The bullish logic for TAO requires you to believe that a game theory miracle can work. But such miracles have happened before in the cryptocurrency industry.

Bittensor possesses one of the most elegant narratives in the cryptocurrency space: a decentralized AI marketplace where market mechanisms allocate funds to the most impactful research. TAO is the coordinating layer, subnets are the labs, and the marketplace is the funding committee.

Strip away the narrative, and you'll find something more unsettling.

Bittensor is a funding program where cryptocurrency speculators fund AI research—and those funded are not obligated to return any value to TAO.

Bittensor is a funding program where cryptocurrency speculators provide funding for AI research—and those funded are not obligated to return any value to TAO.

Think of TAO as Elon Musk—the first investor in OpenAI, a “non-profit” company. Subnets are like Sam Altman—they are the builders who receive funding and deliver the product, but have no contractual obligation to share the profits. They may eventually choose to privatize the profits without returning any value to their original funding sources. Bittensor distributes TAO tokens to subnet operators and miners based on the price of the subnet token. Once a subnet receives a TAO allocation, there is no mandatory mechanism requiring that the AI ​​models, datasets, or services it generates remain within the Bittensor ecosystem. Subnet operators can use Bittensor's TAO incentives to generate revenue and then move the real product elsewhere—to centralized cloud servers, package it as a standalone API, or simply sell it as a SaaS. TAO has no equity or licensing agreement. The only binding is the subnet token—the token price must be maintained to ensure access to resources. But this only works before the subnet "flies out": once the product is strong enough to stand on its own outside the Bittensor system, this tether breaks. The relationship between Bittensor and the subnet is less like venture capital and more like research funding—it gives you start-up capital, but you don't get shares. To put it bluntly, Bittensor is essentially a wealth transfer: from the pockets of token speculators to the accounts of AI researchers—or more bluntly, from retail investors to tech-savvy "miners." The principle is simple: TAO investors are essentially underwriting the entire ecosystem. They buy and hold TAO, supporting the price, which itself serves as a conduit for funds to flow into the subnet incentive system. Subnet operators earn TAO inflation rewards by "demonstrating performance"—but in reality, this "performance demonstration" largely means maintaining a favorable price for their subnet's token. AI products built with this funding can be abandoned at any time—the only constraint is that they still need to continuously acquire network resources. This is the nightmare VCs fear most: you provide the funding, they develop the product, but they owe you nothing. All that's left is a token issuance schedule and a prayer. Now let's look at it from a different angle. The optimistic view rests on two pillars: The persistent need for resources means AI companies always face funding shortages. Computation, data, and talent are expensive. If Bittensor can reliably provide these resources at scale, subnets have a legitimate incentive to remain—not because they are locked up, but because leaving means losing access to these resources. Logically, there's a soft support: AI's demand for resources is endless, and TAO's capacity is unattainable through self-financing alone. Following this logic, the subnet team will proactively maintain its token valuation; without any coercive mechanisms, the TAO economy will spontaneously form a positive flywheel. Cryptocurrencies excel at resource aggregation. Bitcoin aggregates massive computing power solely through token incentives. Ethereum's proof-of-work mechanism has also achieved tremendous success, becoming a powerful magnet for computing resources. Bittensor is applying the same strategy to the field of artificial intelligence. The "enforcement mechanism" is precisely the token game itself—as long as TAO has value, the incentive to participate will continuously increase. If 1000 simulations of Bittensor's future were performed, the distribution of the results would be extremely skewed. In most simulated scenarios, Bittensor will remain a niche funded project. The AI ​​output from subnets will be insignificant. The best-performing subnets will gain significant attention, reap rewards, and then revert to closed-source mode, leaving no value for TAO. When the token supply exceeds the value created, the TAO token will depreciate. In a few simulated paths, something actually works. A subnet creates a truly competitive AI service, and the network effect begins to snowball. TAO becomes a true coordinating layer for decentralized AI infrastructure—not by forcibly capturing value, but by the inherent attraction of being a reserve asset in a functioning AI economy. In very rare cases, TAO becomes the defining asset class of an entirely new entity. Where might problems arise? The bearish logic is simple: There's no stickiness. Once the subnet no longer needs TAO token incentives, it will leave. Bittensor is a transitional phase, not the final destination. Centralized AI has an overwhelming advantage. Companies like OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic possess orders of magnitude in computing power and talent. TAO cannot match the strength of the venture capital and private equity markets. Therefore, the most talented individuals will choose the traditional development path. Issuance is taxation. TAO's issuance plan subsidizes subnets by diluting holders. If the value created by a subnet doesn't match this dilution, it's a slow, painful drain disguised as a "growth mechanism." The optimistic scenario, frankly, is more wishful thinking than a truly viable path to success. The majority of capital invested in TAO will ultimately subsidize development activities that don't return value to token holders. However, Crypto has repeatedly demonstrated that token-incentivized coordinated games can produce outcomes unpredictable by any rational model. Bitcoin shouldn't have succeeded, but it did—although this argument itself is weak, and the industry has used it to endorse numerous projects that don't stand up to first-principles scrutiny. The core problem with TAO isn't whether a coercive mechanism exists—it doesn't, and dTAO's efforts haven't changed that. The core problem is: are the game-theoretic incentives strong enough to keep the best subnets on track? Buying TAO is betting that a "soft guarantee" will hold true in harsh reality. This is either naiveté or foresight.

data.title: Why has Warren Buffett remained wealthy for 60 years? What does a 60% annualized return on Bitcoin mean?
data.description:
Source: "When Shift Happens" podcastCompiled by: Felix, PANews

Cryptocurrency strategist Mark Moss recently appeared on the "When Shift Happens" podcast, where he pointed out that Bitcoin will reach $45 million by 2050 through perspectives such as the 50-year technological revolution cycle, value storage baskets, and the geopolitics of de-dollarization. If it replaces the US dollar as the globally accepted unit of account and becomes a neutral reserve asset, its value could even reach $500 million.

This prediction seems quite audacious, and Moss also provided his analytical basis.

PANews has compiled the highlights of the conversation. Host: Every newcomer to Bitcoin asks themselves the same question: How can I get rich with Bitcoin without relying on luck or falling into scams? People always have a "get rich overnight" mentality. Mark Moss: I think this is a flaw in the fiat currency system. The debt system constantly steals your wealth, while real wage growth doesn't keep up with rising costs. Therefore, people have to take more extreme measures and take further risks in an attempt to offset this loss. Lenin once told Keynes, the father of Keynesian economics, that "the best way to destroy capitalism is to devalue the currency." Through a series of inflations, they can steal wealth at will, and continue to steal until all wealth is gone. The best ways to get rich will be gambling and theft. This is our current situation. Look at our current circumstances; the best way to get rich today is considered to be gambling. For example, "YOLO" (You Only Live Once), putting all your money into memes, or gambling in Las Vegas. This is what people perceive as the best way to get rich today. Or it's theft; unfortunately, whether it's fraud on Wall Street or robbery on the street, theft is rampant. While we are in this environment, I don't think this is the best way to get rich. This might sometimes bring you luck, but the truth is, 75% of lottery winners go bankrupt in less than five years. This proves that relying on luck, gambling, and winning big is not the path to wealth. Musicians and professional athletes who have earned tens or hundreds of millions, or even hundreds of millions of dollars, have ultimately gone bankrupt. So, you might certainly stumble upon some meme stock or cryptocurrency, but the chances of holding onto that wealth are extremely slim. In many cases, it can even ruin their lives because we must develop the ability to hold onto wealth. Although nobody wants to hear it, part of the unavoidable experience is that you have to make money and then lose it 100%. You need to constantly experience gains and losses. That's how you learn to cherish it. Only when you lose it will you learn to value it. Then you'll tell yourself that you must never make the same mistake again. If you only make money by luck once, you will eventually lose it and won't know how to make it back. Host: This point is underestimated. There is no such thing as a "get rich quick" shortcut. The cryptocurrency or Bitcoin field is only a slow path to wealth, or even a strategy for rapid bankruptcy. Mark Moss: I'd like to offer a slight counter-argument. If you prefer, you can consider it a strategy for gradual wealth accumulation. The best way to accumulate wealth in the world has always been the stock market. Of course, the best way to accumulate wealth is to start a business. As a creator, solve problems and provide value to the world. The world's richest people, such as Musk, Bezos, and Bill Gates, have all built companies that solve problems; this is the best way to get rich. After that, we can invest the money we earn in real estate or the stock market to double our wealth. The real estate market and the S&P 500 have created most of the world's wealthy. If you look at their returns over a 30-year period, it's roughly 7% to 8% per year. The world's richest people, like Buffett and Ray Dalio, became wealthy in these markets with returns of 7% to 8%. Host: And now Bitcoin is growing every year. Buffett had about $1 million when he was 31 or 32, which shows that even so, you can still become a billionaire over 60 years. Mark Moss: But I must point out one thing about Buffett and Dalio. I see many people entering this field, wanting to quit their jobs to become full-time investors, thinking that this will make them as rich as they are. But I want to remind everyone that Buffett didn't just get rich through investing; he built a company called Berkshire Hathaway, and he used to work there every day. Ray Dalio did the same; he built a company called Bridgewater Associates and went to work there every day. So they were not only investors but also entrepreneurs, and we need to consider both aspects. Returning to the point I made, they accumulated their wealth in real estate and the S&P 500 market, where annual returns are 7%-8%. Over the past five years, Bitcoin's annualized return has been 85%. Over the past three years, it has averaged 60% annually. So when we say this is a "slow and steady wins the race," other markets only offer 8% returns, while Bitcoin offers 65%. How can that be slow? People are simply in an extremely frenzied gambler's mentality, hoping to get everything in 3 weeks or 3 days. Everyone really needs to calm down. They've lost their proper understanding of life and have no basic concept of what wealth is. I often hear people complaining that Bitcoin is too slow and that they need something that can triple their money in 30 days. Host: Like you said, don't expect a steady return of 3 to 5 times; you'll often end up losing 99%. This overly speculative gambling mentality is truly pathetic. Some call it "financial suicide." Mark Moss: Yes, as Michael Saylor said, everyone should generally keep some risk margin in their portfolio. This goes back to the hierarchy of needs: first, lay a solid foundation to ensure the safety of your capital; if you want to gamble with a portion of your money, that's fine, but you need to diversify it and only use a small portion of your funds. Professional investors understand that we always think from the perspective of risk-adjusted returns. The higher the risk, the higher the potential return, so we invest less money. The lower the risk, the more money we invest. We don't distribute our money evenly. So, if you want to chase those hundredfold returns for thrills, no problem, but you must only use a small portion of your portfolio to do so. Host: Why do people think the price of Bitcoin will stop at $1 million? Mark Moss: This is just the next big round number, but there's no reason for it to stop there. The Congressional Budget Office (CBO) predicts that the global basket of value will reach $1600 trillion in 2030, $3800 trillion in 2040, and approximately $8000 trillion in 2050. I think the actual number could be even higher. At Bitcoin's current growth rate, the compound annual growth rate could average around 30% over the next decade. By 2040, Bitcoin will account for approximately 8% of this value basket. In comparison, Uber and Airbnb gained 10% market share in 10 years. Once it reaches 8%, Bitcoin's price will reach $14 million. By 2050, it could account for about 20% of the basket, which would push Bitcoin's price to a staggering $45 million. Host: That sounds crazy. But I interviewed David Bailey the other day, and he said it's entirely possible that Bitcoin could reach $50 million to $100 million someday. Mark Moss: This is entirely possible. I've done a lot of cycle research, including 4-year, 8-year, 50-year cycles. A technological revolution occurs every 50 years, and we're currently in the sixth. Bitcoin and AI fall right into this cycle. This includes four distinct phases. We can look at the diffusion of this innovation cycle using an S-curve. We're currently in the parabolic phase and should achieve widespread adoption around 2050. Furthermore, for thousands of years, money has typically started as a "collectible." If people believe in it, it becomes a store of value. Only when it possesses attributes like portability and durability can it become "money." If enough people use it, it becomes a unit of account—the final stage. Overlaying these four stages onto a 50-year cycle chart, I believe that by 2050, Bitcoin may become the world's unit of account. From a geopolitical perspective, the US dollar has depreciated by 99% over the past 110 years. What truly pushed the world to the brink was NATO's decision a few years ago to freeze Russia's bank accounts. The confiscation of funds from a nuclear-armed superpower was tantamount to a warning to the world. Therefore, the BRICS countries are attempting to issue their own currencies, and China is cooperating to advance its central bank digital currency (CBDC). Everyone is looking for solutions to break free from the dollar. In a new world of tariff wars and distrust, countries are trying to escape the fiat currency system. The only way for the world to continue functioning is to find a neutral reserve asset, like the gold standard of the past. Bitcoin perfectly fits this requirement. Combining technological advancements and the geopolitical landscape, I believe that by 2050-2060, Bitcoin will become the global unit of account. This means that the basket of value, originally worth $850 trillion, will no longer be denominated in dollars, but in Bitcoin. If we divide this $860 trillion by 21 million Bitcoins, each Bitcoin will be worth $400 to $500 million. Host: Hearing this, even the camera crew looked like they were saying, "Oh no, I need to buy some Bitcoin right now." Mark Moss: I know it sounds crazy, but Bitcoin is programmed to always appreciate. This is because the real wealth of human society (goods and services) is always growing; we are constantly inventing new things and solving new problems. Fiat currency dilutes your share of the world's wealth by printing money, but the total amount of Bitcoin remains constant, so you will never be diluted. As long as I own 1 Bitcoin, I will forever possess 1/21 millionth of the world's wealth, and as the total wealth of the world continues to rise, the purchasing power of Bitcoin will never be diluted. Host: You once said that once you buy Bitcoin, your life will change. Why? Mark Moss: Bitcoin changes your life for many reasons, but ultimately it will begin to change your entire mindset, broadening your perspective on issues over time. The purpose of the fiat currency system is to turn us into consumers, encouraging us to consume continuously. One reason governments create inflation is to stimulate people to spend money to boost the economy, when people should actually be incentivized to save. For example, during the pandemic, people stayed home and saved money, which severely impacted the economy. Governments injected trillions of dollars in stimulus to encourage renewed spending. Our money depreciates too quickly, prompting us to keep consuming because things become more expensive if we wait. However, once we own Bitcoin, we realize that our money can actually buy more goods and services in the future. Under the Bitcoin system, you feel that things will be cheaper in the future. When Bitcoin appreciates at 50% or 60% annually, it changes your cost of capital. You start to think: Do I really need that vacation? Do I really need that new house or new car? Because I can simply keep that money in Bitcoin. Tens of thousands of dollars worth of Bitcoin today could become $1 million in 5 years, and $15 million in 15 years. Would I be willing to spend $100,000 today and sacrifice $15 million in the future? Of course, if it's to buy a beach villa for my children, I absolutely would. I'd be willing to use all the money in the world to create wonderful experiences with my children. But it gives you an extra layer of scrutiny when making all your decisions: "Do I really need this?" This changes our value system. Bitcoin makes you more cautious and discerning, which is a good thing. Host: You mentioned that "my money depreciates very quickly." So how quickly does money in a bank account depreciate? Mark Moss: Much faster than people imagine. Governments report to us the Consumer Price Index (CPI), which tracks the rate of increase in the prices of a basket of goods, and the official target is usually around 2%. Federal Reserve Chairman Powell even hinted that they would abandon this absolute target and allow inflation to rise moderately. But the CPI is just a result. What truly represents the rate at which your bank account funds depreciate is the rate of increase in the money supply. Inflation is like inflating a balloon, increasing the volume of money in circulation. Over the past five years, the global M2 money supply has grown by about 8% annually, and even faster in the United States. Therefore, you will likely suffer a loss of 8% to 10% of purchasing power each year. If you add, for example, a 2% risk premium, then your loss will be around 10% to 12%. This means that any investment I make must yield an annual return of more than 10%. Once you realize you must outpace this 10% inflation barrier, all your business and investment decisions become incredibly clear. If you have 17 assets to invest in, you'll find that 15 of them won't outpace this 10%. So, you'll naturally focus on the two things you can outpace: Nasdaq tech stocks and Bitcoin. Host: Even if the government only aims to steal 2% of our wealth each year, it might not sound like much, but that 2% compounded over several years becomes a significant amount; with a 10% to 15% compound interest rate, your money will shrink by 40% to 50% over several years, which is astonishing. Mark Moss: Yes, this is the power of compound interest. We often think linearly and overlook the power of compound interest. If we make a slight change in our financial mindset, even if it's just saving an extra 10% of our income and investing it for 20 years, that's enough wealth to change the trajectory of our lives. People often look down on saving a few thousand dollars now, thinking it's not worth the time, because they ignore the enormous power that money can unleash when properly preserved and compounded.

data.title: Hackers created 1 billion DOT tokens out of thin air, but only earned $230,000.
data.description:
Author: Zhou, ChainCatcher

On the morning of April 13th, Beijing time, on-chain monitoring platforms successively issued alerts: an abnormal increase in Polkadot bridge assets on the Ethereum network occurred.

According to CertiK's analysis, the attacker submitted a carefully crafted cross-chain request to the HandlerV1 contract on the Ethereum side through Hyperbridge's ISMP protocol, and successfully bypassed the verification mechanism by using a genuine MMR proof that had been accepted by the system in the past.

BlockSec Phalcon subsequently issued a technical alert, classifying the vulnerability as an MMR proof replay vulnerability. According to their analysis, the root cause of the vulnerability lies in the fact that the HandlerV1 contract's replay protection only verifies whether the hash value of a request has been used, but the proof verification process does not bind the submitted request payload to the proof being verified. This logical gap allowed the attacker to replay a historical valid proof and pair it with a newly constructed malicious request. This enabled the attacker to execute the ChangeAssetAdmin operation via the TokenGateway.onAccept() path, transferring administrator and minting permissions for the wrapped DOT contract (address: 0x8d...8F90b8) on Ethereum to an address controlled by the attacker. According to on-chain data, after gaining minting permissions, the attacker minted 1 billion bridged DOT tokens, approximately 2805 times the then-reported circulating supply of the token on Ethereum, which was about 356,000. The attackers then used Odos Router and Uniswap V4 liquidity pools to exchange all their tokens for approximately 108.2 ETH, which they transferred to their external account. Based on the then-current prices, they profited approximately $237,000, with the entire attack incurring only about $0.74 in gas fees. BlockSec Phalcon also mentioned that a previous attack using the same method targeted MANTA and CERE tokens, resulting in a loss of approximately $12,000. The total loss from the two attacks was approximately $242,000. Following the incident, leading South Korean exchanges Upbit and Bithumb announced the suspension of deposit and withdrawal services for DOT and the AssetHub Polkadot network to prevent potential fraudulent deposit risks. Polkadot officials stated that the vulnerability only affects DOT transferred to Ethereum via Hyperbridge; it does not affect DOT assets within the Polkadot ecosystem, nor does it affect DOT transferred via other cross-chain bridges. Polkadot and its parachains, as well as native DOT, remain secure and unaffected. Hyperbridge is currently suspended to investigate the issue. It's worth noting that despite the minting scale reaching 1 billion tokens, the actual losses were far lower than the theoretical figures. Due to the extremely limited on-chain liquidity of wrapped DOT on Ethereum, the concentrated sell-off of 1 billion tokens instantly drove the price of wrapped DOT from $1.22 to $0.00012831, a drop of 99.98%, with the vast majority of tokens unable to be effectively liquidated. According to CoinMarketCap data, the price of the native DOT token was also briefly dragged down by market sentiment, falling by nearly 5% at one point. Users on X bluntly stated that who could have imagined that DOT, once a cross-chain legend alongside Ethereum, would ignite social media in this way? Cross-chain bridges have once again become the "Achilles' heel" of the crypto world; what was once ignored has now become a scene of devastation and lamentation. When 1 billion DOT tokens appeared out of thin air, all technical specifications became worthless. Some users jokingly remarked that low liquidity "saved Polkadot's life" in this incident, limiting actual losses to approximately $237,000. However, while the low liquidity of the bridging asset limited the hacker's profits, it exposed the potential vulnerabilities of the cross-chain interoperability layer. Hyperbridge, developed by Polytope Labs, is a cross-chain interoperability project within the Polkadot ecosystem. It has long used cryptographic proofs to replace multi-signature committees as its core security mechanism, positioning itself as a trust-minimized cross-chain infrastructure. The project has previously emphasized its resistance to common bridging attacks.

However, this incident may indicate that the integrity of the cryptographic proof mechanism itself is not sufficient to guarantee security; the specific implementation logic of the Ethereum-side Gateway contract also constitutes an attack surface.

From a broader perspective, this incident is a microcosm of the continuously severe DeFi security situation since 2026.

Several major attacks have occurred this year, including Venus incurring $2.15 million in bad debt due to price manipulation, Resolve over-minting 80 million USR, and Drift suffering a theft of over $285 million in assets. These attacks employed diverse methods and involved a wide range of sectors. Taking over minting power to issue unlimited amounts of currency is not a new attack pattern. However, Hyperbridge's extremely low liquidity unexpectedly mitigated its losses. According to CertiK data, March alone recorded 46 security incidents with total losses of approximately $39.8 million, the highest monthly record since November 2024. CertiK also points out that the increased frequency of code vulnerability exploitation may be related to the rise of AI-assisted vulnerability discovery tools. This increased attack frequency is also prompting the industry to re-examine the boundaries between security and regulation. In response to the Drift Protocol hack, Circle's Chief Strategy Officer, Dante Disparte, called for protocols, wallets, exchanges, and stablecoin issuers to consider security and accountability as shared obligations. He suggested that DeFi protocols could develop on-chain technical protection measures, referencing the circuit breaker mechanisms of traditional markets, and push for legislation to enshrine property rights and financial privacy protection standards in law before the next major incident.

data.title: Bitcoin in the Strait of Hormuz
data.description:
Written by: Bill Qian Source: X, @bill_qian

Every war is a testing ground for various innovations: tanks on the Somme battlefield in 1916, aircraft carriers in the attack on Pearl Harbor in 1941, atomic bombs over Hiroshima and Nagasaki in 1945, and drones in the Russia-Ukraine conflict in 2022 and the Middle East conflict in 2026. This time in the Strait of Hormuz, cryptocurrency is also participating as an innovation in financial technology—telling us from another perspective that the "censorship resistance" and relative "decentralization" of cryptocurrency have become a convenient way for all parties to trade in chaotic times.

Iranians are about to start using cryptocurrency and RMB for settlement.

I think the probability of using mainstream stablecoins like USDT and USDC is very small, because these are essentially products that the US government can freeze at will. In contrast, Bitcoin is more likely to be adopted because it is currently almost the only payment instrument in the world without "counterparty risk." Some might say that gold is also absent, which is true, but this is no longer the 19th century; no one wants to use physical gold for transactions. Looking at history, we can compare this to the Suez Canal Crisis of 1956 and see how then-Egyptian President Nasser handled the situation without Bitcoin. On July 26, 1956, Nasser announced the nationalization of the Suez Canal Company. The compensation agreement between the Egyptian government and the original shareholders stipulated that although the compensation was denominated in Egyptian pounds, "no less than 40% must be paid in pounds, and the remainder in francs," with the exchange rate pegged to the US dollar parity set by the IMF. Therefore, the entire settlement system remained within the pound sterling and dollar system from beginning to end, because the Egyptians also needed the then-current global "hard currency." Britain, France, and the United States subsequently froze all of Egypt's foreign exchange assets within its borders—including Egyptian pounds sterling frozen in London, totaling approximately £128 million. This money remained frozen for three years. It wasn't until February 28, 1959, that Britain and Egypt signed the Anglo-Egyptian Financial Agreement, gradually resolving the issue. Nasser won the battle on the battlefield, but the monetary battle was bought with money. The deeper consequence was that in 1979, Egypt signed the Camp David Accords, becoming the first Arab country to recognize Israel, and subsequently receiving approximately $2 billion in aid annually from the United States, becoming one of Washington's most important allies in the Middle East. The dollar revenue from the canal was never frozen again—not because Egypt found a way to circumvent the system, but because Egypt chose to join it. This was Nasser's only way out back then, but Iran today cannot choose this path. The story of Iran and the Strait of Hormuz is somewhat different today: Iran can threaten to block the passage, but its oil export revenue, as long as it is settled through SWIFT and the dollar system, is at risk of being frozen at any time. Egypt's solution was submission, but Iran cannot take this path at all. Therefore, Bitcoin is the first tool that truly makes "bypassing" possible because it has no counterparty that can be used for political pressure. Three Iterations of Global Fintech in 70 Years Since World War II Since the establishment of the Bretton Woods system in 1944, global fintech has undergone several stages of development, which can essentially be broken down into three levels: What currency do you use? What settlement system do you use? What front-end application services do you use? **Phase One (1944-1990): The Reshaping of Order Powered by IT Technology; the US Dollar and SWIFT Become the Core of the Post-War Financial Order** The core of this phase was the reshaping of order—the United States' coronation as the "New Roman Empire" and the initial application of digital technology. After World War II, at the monetary level, the world first adopted the US dollar; at the settlement system level, SWIFT (established in 1973, officially launched in 1977) and organizations such as Visa, Mastercard, JCB, and China UnionPay emerged; at the front-end service level, plastic cards appeared—the first credit card was the Diners Club in 1950, and the first debit card was a pilot project by Delaware Bank in 1966. Simultaneously, in 1967, a very cool financial technology emerged globally: the ATM. For a time, Japan even boasted about its level of modernization by stating "how many ATMs per 100,000 people." It's important to note that during this process, the old paradigm didn't completely disappear. In fact, until the 1960s, the traditional "hard currency," the British pound, still accounted for over 50% of global settlements. Therefore, the pace of change was sometimes quite slow. The second phase (1990-2009): At the application layer, internet technology continued to accelerate global financial efficiency, but the underlying structure remained unchanged. From the 1990s onwards, the global monetary system and settlement system remained unchanged, but numerous innovations emerged at the front-end application layer, such as PayPal, Nubank in Brazil, Revolut in Europe, and Ant Financial in China, continuously driving improvements in financial efficiency. The third stage (2009 to present): The birth of Bitcoin, marginal innovation in cryptocurrency technology, and the creation of a new paradigm of 24/7 open finance. Driven by blockchain technology, the birth of Bitcoin in 2009 and the launch of the Ethereum mainnet in 2015 marked milestones in this stage. The key to this round of innovation, in my opinion, is the further development of finance in terms of efficiency and freedom—being able to operate 24/7 and not becoming a political tool for rule-makers (relatively speaking). This innovation is just beginning, similar to the internet penetration rate in 1999. First, at the monetary level: stablecoins emerged, allowing strong currencies to be strategically deployed globally with the help of blockchain. Of course, initially, this "strategic deployment" was spontaneously accomplished by grassroots entrepreneurs. For example, USDT's founder became one of the world's top 20 richest people as a result. Secondly, at the settlement layer: the biggest problems with the SWIFT system are, firstly, the US can shut it down at will, making it a political tool in extreme cases, and these extreme cases are becoming increasingly common; secondly, it's slow, with a single cross-border transfer taking at least 1-3 business days and incurring fees of approximately $15-25. The emergence of the Ethereum blockchain network has become an alternative, providing a 24/7 global clearing and settlement network. Thirdly, at the application layer: this round of innovation has spawned many new entities, from trading, payments, acquiring, and brokerage to new types of digital banks. Almost everything that fintech applications can do, the crypto industry is doing at this stage. Examples include the 24/7 exchange Binance and the stablecoin payment card RedotPay. So, will Iran and the Strait of Hormuz become the new arena for the third generation of global fintech after World War II? Let's wait and see.

data.title: Is Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies finished?
data.description: Unlike many who like to enthusiastically predict the future of Bitcoin during bull markets, and who love to write lengthy pronouncements about how high Bitcoin will rise next month or by the end of the year, I don't particularly enjoy this kind of celebratory euphoria. Instead, I prefer to share my long-held view during bear markets, especially when market sentiment is extremely low and even so-called "industry leaders" are lamenting online: The future of the crypto ecosystem is extremely bright. Temporary darkness and downturns cannot obscure the inherent nature and potential of this ecosystem and this asset. However, very few people can see this underlying potential and strength, especially during downturns, and even fewer believe in it. Therefore, this ecosystem is destined to be cruel. It will eliminate batches of once-glamorous projects and dazzling stars with each wave of downturns and troughs. Ultimately, only a very small number of people will reap significant rewards in this ecosystem. Today I'd like to share a long article recently published by a "veteran insider" titled "Bitcoin and Cryptocurrency Have Reached Their End" (see link at the end of the article for details) and my thoughts on it. This article analyzes various aspects of Bitcoin, including its value anchor, the cost and revenue of Bitcoin mining, the irrational "gambling" nature of the crypto ecosystem, margin and leverage risks in the crypto ecosystem, and the use of crypto technology in stablecoins. It concludes that: "The industry won't disappear, but it will shrink significantly. Demands for censorship-resistant transfers and free trading are real and will persist for a long time, just on a much smaller scale than now. Eventually, the industry will shrink to a size that matches the scale of real capital inflows—when the new inflows just cover the system's operational costs, the industry will reach equilibrium. The market capitalization at that equilibrium may only be a fraction of its current value." My fundamental disagreement with this conclusion lies in the following: The viewpoint argues that the current size of the crypto industry is inflated and not worth its current market value. However, the deeper implication of this viewpoint is that the demand and intrinsic value of "censorship resistance" and "free trade" are not justified by the current market price. Furthermore, I believe this viewpoint is limited to a "centralized" perspective, thus underestimating the value of "decentralization," and consequently, the potential value of censorship resistance and free trade. The core spirit driving "resistance to censorship" and "free trade" can be traced back to the values ​​of classical Western liberalism. Besides the well-known "Mr. Democracy" and "Mr. Science," I believe that classical liberal values ​​are also one of the core elements that have enabled Western capitalist countries, especially the United States, to achieve continuous and brilliant successes in science, finance, and innovation. Crypto technology and crypto assets represent a new path for humanity, recreating a virtual world based on classical liberal values. This historical significance and value has not been recognized by many people to this day. The world shaped by classical liberal values ​​is a fantastical world. To describe it with a phrase I really like, it's "orderly chaos." On the surface, it appears chaotic and even disorganized, but at its core, there is a solid system of rules and constraints. There's a good article online that compares two cases in capitalist countries—the United States and Japan—based on the concept of "orderly chaos" (see link 2 at the end of the article for details). In the United States: on one side, there's the devastated Detroit and the drug-addicted Philadelphia; on the other, there's Wall Street, which influences the world's heartbeat, and Silicon Valley, which leads human technological progress. On one side, Washington is stirring up trouble; on the other, the president is strictly bound by the Constitution. This is a country that always moves forward in waves, alternating between periods of great depression and great prosperity; on one hand, it creates human tragedies, and on the other hand, it occasionally creates miracles. Japan, on the other hand, is the opposite: meticulously maintained streets, orderly citizens, and politicians known for their "gentleness, kindness, respect, frugality, and humility." Even abandoned cities and villages are clean and tidy, as if they were just cleaned yesterday. Cities teeming with drug addicts and devastation, as seen in the United States, are extremely rare. Everything seems so beautiful. But you just feel like something is missing, wondering why disruptive innovation and companies that lead human progress don't emerge, grow, and thrive in droves like they do in the United States. In my opinion, the answer is clear: It's precisely because Japan lacks the influence of classical liberal values, let alone long-term practice in this area. Let's look at the crypto ecosystem; doesn't it look familiar to the US? On one side, there are swindlers, thugs, and lunatics everywhere; on the other side, there are seemingly unbelievable people like Vitalik Buterin. On one side, there are projects riddled with problems—theft, robbery, and "conspiracy"—but on the other side, all of this is exposed in the on-chain world, strongly constrained by smart contracts and cryptographic technology. In the crypto ecosystem, people from all walks of life and those from prestigious universities can sit at the same table and share their innermost thoughts; casino memes and lofty ideals can coexist harmoniously on the blockchain. This is a typical manifestation of "orderly chaos." Although this blockchain world is still in its infancy, and although it is far from perfect in many ways, it increasingly resembles a new world deeply implanted with the genes of classical liberalism. During the Self-Strengthening Movement, Li Hongzhang once borrowed money from a foreign bank. Foreign firms from various countries were scrambling to secure this lucrative deal. However, when the British saw that Li Hongzhang preferred to borrow from an American bank, they were furious and retorted (roughly, "How many years has that country been established?"). They were all swindlers and peasants we didn't want, lacking everything, and in no way could they compare to the British Empire. What was Mr. Li thinking? Yes, the US at that time lacked everything, but it possessed the classical liberal genes that Britain was gradually losing, so it was only a matter of time before it surpassed the British Empire. Similarly, how many years has today's crypto world existed? Compared to other mature virtual ecosystems, it lacks everything, but it possesses the classical liberal genes that no other virtual ecosystem has. Therefore, it is without a doubt that it will stand out in the future and create a vast and unique space. Its potential and value are by no means as pessimistic or narrow-minded as the previous viewpoints suggest.
